items.myth: Gaussian Naive Bayes requires gradient descent training.
items.reality: Training is closed-form maximum-likelihood estimation of class priors and per-class per-feature means and variances — a single pass over the data. No loss minimization loop is required.
items.example: GaussianNB().fit(X, y) computes theta_ and var_ from group statistics, unlike LogisticRegression's iterative solver.

items.myth: Because it uses Bayes' theorem, GNB always produces well-calibrated probabilities.
items.reality: Posteriors are only as good as the naive Gaussian generative assumptions. Violated independence or non-Gaussian features yield miscalibrated P(y|x) even when accuracy is decent.
items.example: Skewed income within each class: fitted μ and σ² miss tail behavior, so high posteriors may not match observed frequencies.

items.myth: GNB and logistic regression are interchangeable on the same numeric data.
items.reality: GNB is generative (models P(x|y) with Gaussian likelihoods); logistic regression is discriminative (models P(y|x) with a sigmoid). They optimize different objectives and fail under different assumption breaks.
items.example: On Iris, both often score high accuracy, but only GNB exposes per-class per-feature means in theta_.

items.myth: The Gaussian PDF value at x must be ≤ 1 because it is a probability.
items.reality: A PDF can exceed 1; only the integral equals 1. GNB uses density values (or their logs) as likelihood factors, not probabilities of exact equality.
items.example: N(x; μ=0, σ²=0.01) has peak density 1/√(2π·0.01) ≈ 3.99 at x=0 — valid PDF, not a probability mass.
